summary: Hotz argues that while Rich Sutton's Bitter Lesson correctly criticizes hand-coding domain knowledge (like 'cone detection' in self-driving cars), it shouldn't be taken to the extreme of evolving AI from scratch. Since our goal is building superhuman AI, not recreating the origin of life, we should reverse-engineer the human brain's architecture rather than letting evolution rediscover it. The brain's components—neocortex, hippocampus, basal ganglia, amygdala, thalamus—are 'hacks' we can replicate with current technology like transformers and TD-learning.
key_points: The Bitter Lesson is correct that hand-coded domain knowledge (cone detection, traffic rules) is ridiculous—humans learn these from data, so AI should too
But taking the lesson to its extreme (evolving AI from scratch like evolution did) is unnecessary since we have a working reference implementation: the human brain
Building AI is like building a radio—reverse-engineering an existing one is faster than recreating the conditions that led to its invention
Human brain architecture (neocortex, hippocampus, etc.) should guide AI architecture, mapping to transformers, RAG, TD-learning, etc.
The goal is 'knock-off humans' that can then solve harder problems—we don't need to solve life itself
money_quotes: Humanity only has one engineering project, building better engineers than humans. After that, the thing we built can do the engineering.
Obviously there's no reference to traffic cones inside human DNA, they learn about them from data. So there shouldn't be any reference to traffic cones in your codebase.
The brain isn't some hyperelegant machine that captures the essence of learning, it's a bunch of hacks, and hacks that we can replicate.
I say the bitter lesson stops at human DNA.
We are just trying to build knock-off humans, not solve life. They can do that.
Having a cone guy in your self driving car company is still ridiculous, because the only working implementation of a driving agent doesn't have a cone guy, it learns cones from data.
key_insight: The Bitter Lesson's rejection of hand-coded knowledge should stop at human brain architecture—we should reverse-engineer the brain's modular structure rather than trying to evolve intelligence from scratch.
